cases.title: The Silent Room
cases.presentation: Ten minutes into a code, the resuscitation room has gone quiet except for the monitor beeps. The team leader realizes no one has spoken up about the current rhythm or plan in over a minute.
cases.question: What team dynamics problem does this best illustrate?
options: A: The team is functioning efficiently with no issues.
B: A breakdown in communication — silence often signals lost situational awareness, not smooth teamwork.
C: The patient has achieved ROSC.
D: Compression quality is guaranteed to be adequate.
cases.explanation: A quiet resuscitation is a warning sign, not a reassurance. Active closed-loop communication, frequent reevaluation, and verbalized plans keep the whole team's mental model synchronized; silence often means the team has lost track of the plan.
cases.clinicalPearl: A quiet code is not a calm code — silence is a red flag for lost situational awareness.

cases.title: Airway Just Placed
cases.presentation: A supraglottic airway is successfully placed and confirmed during ongoing chest compressions. The rescuer bagging the patient continues to pause compressions every 30 compressions to deliver 2 breaths.
cases.question: What correction should the team leader make?
options: A: Continue the current 30:2 pattern — it is correct with any airway.
B: Direct the team to continuous compressions with 1 breath every 6 seconds now that the airway is placed.
D: Increase breaths to every 2 seconds.
cases.explanation: Once an advanced airway (supraglottic or endotracheal) is placed and confirmed, compressions become continuous and breaths are delivered every 6 seconds independent of the compression cycle — 30:2 cycling should stop.
cases.clinicalPearl: Airway in, cycle out — advanced airway means continuous compressions plus a breath every 6 seconds.

cases.title: The Overlooked Error
cases.presentation: The team leader orders a second dose of amiodarone, apparently forgetting the first dose was given 2 minutes ago. A pharmacist on the team recognizes the mistake but hesitates, unsure whether to speak up to the attending physician leading the code.
cases.question: What is the correct action?
options: A: Say nothing since the physician is the team leader.
B: Respectfully and promptly speak up to clarify the dosing history (constructive intervention).
C: Quietly give the dose without comment.
D: Wait until after the code to mention it in private.
cases.explanation: Constructive intervention means any team member — regardless of role or hierarchy — should speak up immediately and respectfully when they notice a potential error, to protect patient safety.
cases.clinicalPearl: Hierarchy should never silence a safety concern — speak up promptly and respectfully.
